author: @killzilla
text:
hello Robert, thanks for the honnest aproach in all your vids!
I have a question: lets say I have a wind tunnel in a 3mx3mx10m structure with constant wind speed of 2,5 to 3m/s maybe even 4m/s sometimes, 24/7 with no flow issues, just nice horizontal direct flow. is there any cost efficient turbine that can fit in there? 
I have been watching some of  your vids, and something like the daisy comes to mind
it just seems such a waste of energy, its not strong wind, but its allways there 24/7. thanks again Robert

author: @MichaelAckerman
text: Isn't this an inverse square surface? The period of the planets follow an inverse square rule. [Edit] y=-1/(xx) . Plug it into Wolfram Alpha to see a graph, which resembles the cross section of a coin funnel. A hyperbolic surface is y=-1/|x| which looks much the same, but that absolute value seems like a fudge.

author: @whatelseison8970
text: Another name for that shape is a pseudosphere; so named because it exhibits constant _negative_ gaussian curvature, whereas for a sphere it is positive.  Yet another name is a tractricoid because it is the surface of revolution of the tractrix, a curve of pursuit.  If, for example, you were to start pushing a bicycle forward starting with the front wheel at 90 degrees, the curve traced by the rear wheel would be a tractrix as the steering straightened back out.  It is the involute of the catenary.

author: @markmetzger5430
text: Absolutely brilliant Rob. I have been thinking exactly along these same lines for a couple of projects I have in mind and the best I could think of was turning glued foam sheets on an powered spindle. Doable but very messy, labor intensive and not easily scaled up. Your solution is much simpler and effective and can be scaled to almost any size. Thank you for such amazing suggestions and techniques. Cheers mate .

replies.author: @OwnGrid
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering a gravity well will form a double helix shooting at the nozzle scavenging pressure from flow

replies.author: @marksmith9218
replies.text: @OwnGridLike schaubergers home generator you mean? Have you experimented with this? It looks complicated to construct, the spiral arms are perhaps intended to create and extract cavitation in the vortex flow at the nozzles. There is a more simple device patented called the Donnie watts generator.

replies.author: @OwnGrid
replies.text: @marksmith9218 Yes vortex flow and special nozzles are not easy to fabricate, but 3d printing also negative casting if metals are needed is much easier nowadays, and  definitely in the future will play with that. Thank you for letting me know about this generator it looks interesting

replies.author: @marksmith9218
replies.text: @OwnGrid Yes take a look at the pistol shrimp, a BBC nature clip, here on YouTube, I believe it's nature's version of such a vortex nozzle under water. It seems that schauberger was probably using cavitation implosions in his machines. Also take a look at the Clem engine, an oil version of this type of machine.

author: @harliethomas1378
text: This really tugs at my brain about dwell time and energy extraction from a turbine. Kind of like if you give squirrel to liquid in a jug or a 2 liter bottle that is the fastest way to drain the liquid. I often uses when dumping fluid out of containers or pouring oil into a funnel into a car. Rather than straight down having an offset to the center so it's Swirls and goes in faster. Now I know what's tugging at my brain a de Laval nozzle. I am going to have to cut up one of the old 5 gallon water bottles and maybe put a lily impeller in the neck and see what happens. This is gonna make me say up one of my 3d printers soon now

author: @peterlang777
text: schauberger vortex tech is being used to make a hydrogen plant in new Hampshire.  company is qhydrogen

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting

replies.author: @peterlang777
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering the technology is "waveform discs"  that are schauberger impeller rotors in series. some proprietary metallurgy involved too. they are building a plant in groveton new hampshire. the CEO is irvin Whitaker

replies.author: @marksmith9218
replies.text: @peterlang777 Thanks for this, I had a look at Whitakers patents and it's almost identical to schaubergers repulsine right? There is also a patent for a thrust engine using the same technology. But will big oil allow it to see the light of day? Probably not unfortunately!

replies.author: @peterlang777
replies.text: @marksmith9218 here is the funny thing ! whitaker is 3rd generation big oil money!!!  his father was obsessed with this tech and worked on it for years.  anyone who sees his patents and knows of the repulsine says the same thing. they are very obviously based on them.

replies.author: @peterlang777
replies.text: he isnt the only one. hunt petroleum of texas has moved in a very big way  to perovskite solar. they are a very old oil company privately held as a family biz. for a century.  even the saudis are moving to green hydrogen "mene mene tikal upharsin" (the writing on the wall)
